text: The surgeon begins a robotic sigmoid resection for diverticulitis by suspending the uterus to improve visibility of the pelvis. The sigmoid colon is adhered to the left pelvis, requiring exposure to free up a robotic arm. Adhesions in the pelvic area are visible, and the surgeon plans to preserve the superior hemorrhoidal artery while performing a lateral to medial dissection.
The surgeon lifts the sigmoid mesentery off the retroperitoneum, revealing the left ureter. The sigmoid colon is visibly adhered to the left pelvic sidewall near the iliac vessels and left adnexa.

The surgeon demonstrates proximal mobilization of the sigmoid colon, which is adhered to the left pelvic sidewall. The thickened rectal mesentery is visible as the surgeon attempts to elevate the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis.

The surgeon frees the distal epiploic appendages of the sigmoid from the rectal mesentery to elevate it out of the pelvis. The distal sigmoid is further freed to visualize the planned transection margin on the rectum. The surgeon preserves the superior hemorrhoidal artery while dissecting the long, floppy mesentery along the bowel wall to the proximal margin.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the thickened mesentery at the distal margin, performing multiple firings to ensure complete separation.

The surgeon works towards the upper rectum, cleaning the area to better visualize the distal target. The inflamed tissue is secondary to the sigmoid colon resting on top. The surgeon uses ICG to check margins, marking them for visibility under white light, before proceeding to check the distal margin and perform the transection.

The surgeon performs a sewn single-layer anastomosis, starting posteriorly in the middle and sewing out to the far side on the left. The procedure involves taking full-thickness bites with Velox sutures, ensuring a good size match between the proximal and distal bowel. The anterior wall is nicely imbricated, and the needle is parked before introducing a second one.

The surgeon performs a single-layer anastomosis, taking full thickness bites to ensure secure suturing beyond the cut serosa. After assessing a previous bite as thin, the surgeon revisits the area to take a larger bite, continuing the suturing around the bowel. The technique involves cannelling over the top and ending sutures in the middle rather than at the ends.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test using a rigid scope through the anastomosis. The anastomosis is widely patent with no visible bleeding or bubbles.
